findings: Added executable check-readme-release to enforce bilingual README mirror, text-to-image hero provenance, README model evidence, version consistency, command-surface wording, and public-boundary checks.
Added audit-installed-skills so installed user-created skills can be rechecked with deep contract logic without ad hoc shell scripts.
Fixed global route scoring so README/hero/bilingual tasks route to readme-showcase-writer even when the target project name is SkillGuard.
Recompiled skillguard-global-router's source work contract with deep fields and created a current run record after installed audit found its old shallow contract.
Synchronized installed SkillGuard and SkillGuard Global Router copies to the source skill directories and removed explicitly identified extra old run-record files.
Installed audit returned 64 audited user skills with deep-pass:64 after the fix.
friction_points: The first check-readme-release implementation treated a PNG as UTF-8 text while building file evidence; it was corrected to record image byte_count and hash.
A PowerShell Copy-Item call used -LiteralPath with a wildcard and did not copy; it was rerun with explicit child enumeration.

Global route resolution initially selected skillguard for a README task because the project name appeared in the request; the route scoring rule now suppresses the SkillGuard-specific bias for README tasks.

commands: python ~/.codex/skills/skillguard/scripts/skillguard.py audit-installed-skills --root ~/.codex/skills --output -; parse each contract for target_rule_inventory, route_inventory, workflow_stage_inventory, native_check_inventory, coverage_matrix, phase_native_bindings, parallel flag, and run-record policy
findings: Sequential covered-skill audit produced one row per covered installed user skill, not only an aggregate test result.

All 64 covered installed user skills had deep-pass, complete target-lock status, non-empty target rules, non-empty route inventory, non-empty workflow stage inventory, non-empty coverage matrix, and may_define_parallel_execution_route=false.
Only skillguard and skillguard-global-router remained skillguard-runtime with run_record_required=true; native-integrated and hybrid-extension skills retained run_record_required=false.

FlowPilot specifically remained native-integrated with 45 source requirements, 45 target rules, 6 route inventory rows, 1 workflow-stage inventory row, 16 native checks, 45 coverage matrix rows, 5 phase native bindings, parallel_allowed=false, and run_record_required=false.
friction_points: The first table display truncated right-side columns and used a reserved PowerShell variable name; it was rerun as CSV with all relevant columns visible.
next_actions: Continue release workflow only after git diff/privacy review confirms no stale or private artifacts are staged.
risk_evidence_summary: This sequential audit confirms coverage depth by contract content counts, not by merely rerunning the test suite.

Each covered skill row was checked for source requirements, target rules, route inventory, workflow stages, native checks, coverage matrix, phase bindings, parallel route flag, run-record policy, and ok status.
skipped_steps: The full per-skill CSV was reviewed in-terminal and summarized here; it was not committed as a public evidence artifact to avoid publishing local installed-skill inventory details.

findings: Reran installed FlowPilot check-depth after peer-agent activity and confirmed it is native-integrated, not a SkillGuard-owned parallel route.

Parsed FlowPilot's installed contract fields directly: 45 source requirements, 45 target rules, 6 route rows, 1 workflow-stage row, 16 native checks, 45 coverage matrix rows, 5 phase native bindings, may_define_parallel_execution_route=false, may_define_skillguard_runtime_route=false, run_record_required=false.
Reran installed audit and parsed all 64 covered skill contracts: deep-pass=64, bad_rows=0, hybrid-extension=17, native-integrated=45, skillguard-runtime=2.
Direct checks for readme-showcase-writer and frontend-design passed, confirming README-specific bilingual/hero/model gates and UI-design target rules are present in their own contracts.
Global prompt freshness check passed against registry hash 7F9792ABD7EC99E3068EAD5DE0483D75DEE2FC88B7ABC350A866C95B8B49CB3C.
friction_points: The aggregate installed audit alone was not treated as enough evidence; the follow-up parser inspected each target contract's content fields to prove depth rather than only test success.
Parallel agents can still change installed skill files after a passing check, so the release workflow must repeat installed audit immediately before commit/release.

counterexamples: OpenSpec verify initially failed because the verification contract ran SkillGuard repo commands from the planning root with the wrong relative paths; the contract was corrected to execute the real SkillGuard repository checks.
findings: check-depth now treats deep-pass as target-specific semantic coverage and requires evidence ids in coverage rows instead of accepting generic field presence alone.

OpenSpec verify passed after the verification contract was corrected; the earlier path failure is recorded as a verification-contract issue, not an implementation pass.
friction_points: The formal verification contract had to be corrected because OpenSpec runs from the planning root while SkillGuard CLI resolves target arguments from the SkillGuard repository root.
Large JSON command output is truncated in the terminal; key release evidence is summarized as counts and pass/fail status instead of committing local installed-skill inventory details.

commands.command: FlowGuard V1 retirement and renewal scenario review
commands.summary: 7 legal scenarios passed; 23 expected violations observed; zero misses or oracle mismatches
7 v2-only, 15 v1-legacy, zero blocked or mixed targets
exact former V1 authority surfaces removed transactionally
SkillGuard renewed through sequence 5 without restoring V1
every declared positive passed and every declared shallow case blocked
current final parent evidence required before closure
494 SkillGuard files and 34 router files reached exact staged parity and smoke pass
current project adoption and suite inventory passed

counterexamples: A one-point or wrong-stratum time series cannot satisfy target-owned temporal depth.
A retired V2 target with reintroduced V1 runtime files resolves blocked.
A failed completion-renewal write restores every touched path and never recreates V1.
A broad transient filter that removed nested fixture runs caused stage parity failure and was narrowed to the member control root.
findings: Seven complete V2 targets formally retired V1 runtime authority; fifteen ungraduated targets retain V1 as their sole explicit v1-legacy authority.
Universal execution depth remains target-neutral in SkillGuard while target skills own routes, domain semantics, coverage universes, per-object floors, strata, and native checks.
Post-retirement maintenance uses a short content-addressed, monotonic completion chain rather than relaxed freshness or restored V1.
Project prompts in all six repositories contain one managed SkillGuard block and the canonical SkillGuard repository URL.
friction_points: Initial full regressions exposed stale tests and fixtures that still addressed retired SkillGuard V1 paths.
Windows legacy path length required a short content-addressed renewal history directory.
The project adoption log suggested scripts/verify_skill_suite_markers.py, but that script is not present in SkillGuard; current python -m flowguard project-audit supplied the available suite audit.
metadata: This record proves modeled retirement/renewal behavior, current authority classification, named executable checks, and staged installation evidence only. It does not prove target-domain correctness, future AI behavior, release, publication, or arbitrary project-level simulation accuracy.
next_actions: Activate only after the current full TestMesh and OpenSpec verification pass.
Rerun installed 22-target authority audit after transactional synchronization.
risk_evidence_summary: Formal retirement evidence is separate from domain-depth evidence.
Skipped or failed checks remain visible and are never counted as pass.
skipped_steps: Git commit, push, tag, GitHub release, and public publication were not authorized and were not performed.
No migration claim is used as proof of PhysicsGuard model accuracy or other target-domain correctness.
task_summary: Formally retire V1 runtime authority from eligible Guard targets while preserving universal deep-use enforcement and bounded V1-only targets.
trigger_reason: The user explicitly requested formal migration and deletion instead of retaining a generic old-version compatibility layer.
